title: Fathom vs Corcoran: Which Brokerage is Best for Realtors?
excerpt: If you’re a real estate agent evaluating your next move, this comparison between Fathom Realty and Corcoran lays out exactly what you need to know.…
content:
If you’re a real estate agent evaluating your next move, this comparison between Fathom Realty and Corcoran lays out exactly what you need to know. These brokerages operate with fundamentally different approaches when it comes to commission, support, technology, and overall agent experience. Whether you’re trying to keep more of your money or build prestige in luxury markets, here’s how they stack up.

Commission Splits and Fees
Income Opportunities
Training and Support
Technology and Resources
Agent Satisfaction
Final Thoughts on Fathom vs. Corcoran
Why Smart Agent Alliance Agents Thrive at eXp Realty

Commission Splits and Fees

Fathom Realty offers a 100/0 commission split, meaning agents keep every dollar of commission they earn. There are no franchise or royalty fees, and the only out-of-pocket costs are a flat $700 per year and per-transaction fees between $150 and $250. This ultra-simple structure is ideal for agents who want to calculate their take-home pay without mental gymnastics.

Corcoran takes the opposite approach. Commission splits range from 50/50 to 70/30, and agents also pay a 6% franchise fee on every transaction. There’s no production cap, which means that even top-producing agents continue to split commissions and pay fees indefinitely. Transaction fees are wrapped into that structure, but Corcoran also charges separate costs for E&O insurance—often on the higher end. Monthly office fees vary by location, adding more inconsistency to an agent’s cost structure.

If your goal is maximizing profit, Fathom offers clarity and savings. Corcoran, while built for luxury branding, definitely takes a bigger cut.

Income Opportunities

Fathom Realty is strictly transactional. There’s no revenue share, no stock awards, and no pathway to earn retirement or willable income through the company. You earn what you sell—full stop. That’s great if you’re focused on commissions today but offers no passive income potential for the future.

Corcoran operates similarly. There are no residual income paths, equity awards, or wealth-building opportunities beyond traditional sales. Like Fathom, you sell, you earn, and that’s where the income stops.

Neither brokerage supports agents in building long-term financial freedom. If you want your income to grow beyond what you personally produce, you’ll have to look elsewhere.

Training and Support

Fathom Realty keeps things lean, which means there’s no formal in-house training and no around-the-clock agent support. This model works well for seasoned agents who are already operating independently, but may leave newer agents feeling a little stranded.

Corcoran does provide training, but the quality and structure vary significantly depending on the local office. There’s no universal system, and like Fathom, there’s no 24/7 support, so you’re dependent on office availability and manager responsiveness. It’s workable if you’re in a high-performing office, but it’s inconsistent across the board.

For both brokerages, self-motivation is a must. You won’t find structured national programs or on-demand mentorship platforms here.

Technology and Resources

Fathom Realty offers a tech platform that covers the basics—transaction management, client communication, and compliance tools. It gets the job done, but it’s not designed to impress. If you want cutting-edge marketing automation, AI-powered CRMs, or integrated branding systems, you’ll need to add your own tools or join a team that provides them.

Corcoran, with its luxury real estate focus, does provide high-end marketing resources. Presentation matters here, and the materials reflect that. Their technology isn’t necessarily more advanced, but it’s geared toward the polished look and feel expected in luxury markets. Still, it’s not a cloud-based platform and doesn’t offer the kind of flexibility or innovation you’ll find with more modern models.

In short, Fathom gives you the freedom to build with lean tools. Corcoran provides stronger branding, but the tech behind it may not evolve as fast as today’s most scalable brokerages.

Agent Satisfaction

Fathom Realty has earned a solid 4.6-star rating from hundreds of agent reviews on Glassdoor. Agents regularly praise the simplicity of the model and the ability to keep more of their income. Common frustrations center around the lack of training and the self-reliance required to succeed.

Corcoran comes in slightly behind with a 4.4-star rating. Agents often speak highly of the company’s luxury brand and marketing support but also note concerns about high fees and commission splits that chip away at earnings. Again, office-by-office experiences vary, and that inconsistency shows up in the reviews.

Both brokerages have their loyal fans—but the financial structure at Fathom tends to generate more consistent praise.

Final Thoughts on Fathom vs. Corcoran

Fathom Realty is a match for agents who want to maximize their commission, eliminate unnecessary fees, and run their business without extra layers. It’s not for agents who need hand-holding or crave the cachet of a big-name brand, but it’s built for independence and profitability.

Corcoran is a fit for agents committed to the luxury space who value strong marketing assets and a prestigious brand. However, you’ll pay for that brand recognition through lower splits and higher fees—and you won’t get much in return when it comes to wealth-building options or passive income.

If you’re just comparing these two, the question becomes: do you want to be branded or be profitable?

Why Smart Agent Alliance Agents Thrive at eXp Realty

title: Fathom vs Better Homes: Which is Best for Realtors?
excerpt: This isn’t just another brokerage comparison — it’s a real look at how your paycheck, support, and business tools will be affected by your choice.…
content:
This isn’t just another brokerage comparison — it’s a real look at how your paycheck, support, and business tools will be affected by your choice. Whether you’re drawn to the simplicity of flat fees or the prestige of a well-known name, here’s how Fathom Realty and Better Homes & Gardens Real Estate compare where it counts.

Commission Splits and Fees
Income Opportunities
Training and Support
Technology and Resources
Agent Satisfaction
Final Thoughts on Fathom vs. Better Homes & Gardens
Why Smart Agent Alliance Agents Thrive at eXp Realty

Commission Splits and Fees

At Fathom Realty, the 100/0 commission split means what it says — agents keep every cent of their commission. There are no franchise or royalty fees taking a cut, and agents enjoy complete freedom from production caps. The only recurring costs are a flat $700 annual fee and a per-transaction fee between $150 and $250. This predictable, no-frills model has been one of Fathom’s biggest selling points for agents who want to simplify their finances and scale their business without penalty.

Better Homes & Gardens, in contrast, operates under a traditional split model that ranges from 60/40 to 80/20. The company also takes a 6% franchise or royalty fee, which further trims the agent’s net income. There’s no cap in place, so even top producers continue paying the split. Monthly fees vary based on office and region, and while transaction fees are baked into the royalty structure, there are additional costs like E&O insurance. Overall, it’s a higher-cost model, even though it comes with the familiarity of a household brand.

If your goal is to keep more of what you earn, Fathom’s lean model clearly gives you more breathing room.

Income Opportunities

Fathom Realty doesn’t offer revenue share, equity awards, or retirement paths — it’s a commission-only setup. What you close is what you make, and that’s where it ends. It’s clean and simple, but there’s no added value for long-term wealth building.

Better Homes & Gardens follows the same philosophy. Agents earn their commission and have no access to recurring income, revenue sharing, or other wealth-building incentives. So while the company supports sales activity with its branding, it doesn’t reward agents beyond the deals they personally close.

Neither model offers additional income streams, which may work fine for those focused only on transactions, but won’t meet the needs of agents thinking about scaling or exiting the business someday.

Training and Support

Fathom Realty has earned a reputation for being agent-friendly, but it doesn’t provide structured training or 24/7 support. That approach works well for confident, experienced agents who already know the ropes, but may feel limiting to newer agents seeking mentorship or coaching. The low overhead and minimal intrusion are perks for self-starters, but not ideal for someone who needs handholding.

Better Homes & Gardens does offer training, but it varies by office. Some locations are known for robust support and onboarding, while others offer the bare minimum. Like Fathom, there’s no 24/7 support, so agents are largely dependent on local managers and office culture for help.

In both models, success depends heavily on your own initiative and your specific office environment.

Technology and Resources

Fathom Realty gives agents access to basic tech tools like transaction management systems and CRMs. It’s not flashy, but it gets the job done. There’s no massive tech suite, and agents who want advanced lead generation or marketing platforms will need to build that stack independently or join a team that offers it.

Better Homes & Gardens leans into technology and branding more heavily. With the backing of a recognizable brand, agents can access strong marketing resources and CRM systems designed to support high visibility and lead follow-up. These tools can be especially useful for agents who are less tech-savvy or who want to plug into a ready-made system.

Fathom provides the essentials with freedom to customize. Better Homes & Gardens offers a more fully built platform, but it comes at a higher cost.

Agent Satisfaction

Fathom Realty currently holds a 4.6-star rating on Glassdoor, with hundreds of agents praising the simple commission structure, predictable fees, and freedom to operate independently. Agents regularly mention financial clarity as one of the biggest advantages.

Better Homes & Gardens earns a slightly lower rating of 4.3 stars from fewer total reviews. Agents tend to appreciate the branding, marketing tools, and name recognition, but also mention the pain of ongoing fees and limited income options as recurring downsides.

If agent reviews are any indicator, the satisfaction difference often comes down to expectations — whether agents prioritize branding or bottom-line income.

Final Thoughts on Fathom vs. Better Homes & Gardens

Fathom Realty is the choice for agents who want full commission, fixed fees, and minimal brokerage interference. It’s best suited for those who value autonomy and know how to manage their business without needing constant support or tech-heavy tools.

Better Homes & Gardens makes more sense for agents who want the credibility of a national brand and access to advanced marketing resources. But it comes with the trade-off of lower commission retention and more ongoing costs, with no cap in sight.

Neither brokerage supports passive income, equity, or long-term wealth strategies. If that’s what you’re looking for, you may want to explore an alternative that gives you both earning power and financial growth opportunities.

Why Smart Agent Alliance Agents Thrive at eXp Realty

title: LPT vs REMAX: Which Brokerage is Best for Realtors in 2025?
excerpt: In this comparison between LPT vs REMAX, we’ll dive into the key aspects that agents should consider, including commission structures, fees, income opportunities, and agent…
content:
In this comparison between LPT vs REMAX, we’ll dive into the key aspects that agents should consider, including commission structures, fees, income opportunities, and agent support.

Commission Splits and Fees
Income Opportunities
Training and Support
Technology and Resources
Agent Satisfaction
Final Thoughts on LPT  vs REMAX
Join Our FREE Team at eXp Realty

Commission Splits and Fees

LPT Realty offers commission splits ranging from 80/20 to 100/0. This flexibility allows agents to maximize their earnings. There are no franchise or royalty fees, making it more cost-effective for agents who want to keep a higher percentage of their commissions. The production cap at LPT Realty is $15K, meaning agents keep 100% of their commissions after hitting this cap. Monthly fees vary, with options as low as $500/year plus $0 or $89/month, or $149/month, depending on the agent’s choice. Transaction fees are $195 before reaching the cap.

In contrast, RE/MAX has commission splits that start at 60/40 and go up to 95/5. However, RE/MAX charges a 5% franchise/royalty fee, which reduces agents’ take-home earnings. The production cap is significantly higher at $23K, and monthly fees range from $300 to $2,500, making RE/MAX a more costly option for agents. Transaction fees are included in the royalty fees, which may provide some predictability, but the higher fees overall can reduce the agent’s bottom line.

Income Opportunities

LPT Realty stands out by offering a revenue share program, allowing agents to earn additional income through team building and recruitment. This offers an extra stream of passive income beyond commissions. LPT also provides retirement income and willable income paths, offering agents a long-term financial security option.

RE/MAX, on the other hand, does not offer a revenue share program, nor does it provide retirement or willable income paths. Agents at RE/MAX rely solely on their commission-based income, which may limit long-term financial planning options.

Training and Support

LPT Realty does not have a specified formal training program but offers 24/7 agent support, ensuring that agents have access to help when needed. This level of support is crucial for agents who prefer having consistent access to resources and guidance.

RE/MAX provides access to its Online University for training, which is a structured learning platform. However, it lacks 24/7 support, which may be a downside for agents who need more immediate assistance, particularly in fast-paced markets.

Technology and Resources

LPT Realty provides agents with access to a variety of technology tools and platforms designed to enhance their marketing and client management efforts. The cloud-based model allows for flexibility and efficiency, especially for agents on the go.

RE/MAX also invests in technology, offering agents tools for lead management, marketing, and transaction handling. However, the higher cost structure may make these tools less attractive compared to LPT Realty’s more affordable options.

Agent Satisfaction

LPT Realty has a 4.3-star rating on Glassdoor, with 37 reviews. Agents appreciate the flexibility in commission splits, lower fees, and income opportunities through the revenue share model.

RE/MAX holds a 4.2-star rating from 3.3K reviews. While the brand recognition and vast network are major benefits, agents often mention the high fees as a downside.

Final Thoughts on LPT vs REMAX

LPT Realty offers more flexibility with commission splits, lower fees, and additional income streams through its revenue share program, retirement income path, and willable income options. This makes it a great choice for agents who want to maximize their income potential while keeping their costs low.

RE/MAX, on the other hand, is a well-established brand with strong name recognition and a large network, but its higher fees and lack of additional income opportunities may make it less attractive for agents focused on long-term financial growth.

For more in-depth comparisons, check out our eXp vs traditional brokerage comparison and online brokerage comparison pages. Explore the advantages each brokerage offers, and don’t forget to see why many agents are turning to eXp Realty for even more opportunities.

title: LPT vs Compass: Which is Best for Realtors?
excerpt: In this comparison between LPT  vs Compass, we’ll dive into the key differences that real estate agents should consider when choosing the best brokerage to…
content:
In this comparison between LPT  vs Compass, we’ll dive into the key differences that real estate agents should consider when choosing the best brokerage to meet their career and income goals.

Commission Splits and Fees
Income Opportunities
Training and Support
Technology and Resources
Agent Satisfaction
Final Thoughts on LPT Realty vs Compass
Join Our FREE Team at eXp Realty

Commission Splits and Fees

LPT Realty offers competitive commission splits ranging from 80/20 to 100/0, with no franchise or royalty fees. Agents benefit from a $15K production cap, after which they retain 100% of their commission. LPT Realty’s monthly fees are flexible, starting at $0 and going up to $149, depending on the agent’s package. Transaction fees are set at $195 before the cap, making it a cost-efficient option for agents looking to maximize earnings.

Compass, in contrast, has commission splits that range from 60/40 to 92.5/7.5, however, Compassagents only sometimes have a production cap, meaning agents continue to split commissions indefinitely. Monthly fees start at $145 and can vary depending on the office, and transaction fees may go up to 4%, making it a more costly option.

Income Opportunities

LPT Realty offers a revenue share model, allowing agents to generate passive income by recruiting others to the brokerage. This program creates additional long-term income opportunities for agents. LPT Realty also offers a retirement income path and willable income, ensuring agents can plan for their financial future beyond commissions.

Compass does not offer a revenue share model, retirement income, or willable income paths. Agents at Compass are limited to commission-based income, which can restrict long-term financial growth and wealth-building potential.

Training and Support

LPT Realty provides agents with solid training resources and 24/7 agent support, ensuring agents have the tools they need to succeed at any time. The availability of support round-the-clock is a key advantage for agents who need immediate assistance.

Compass has a more traditional training approach through the Compass Academy, offering good resources but without 24/7 agent support. This can be a downside for agents who require ongoing support, especially during non-standard working hours.

Technology and Resources

LPT Realty provides advanced technology platforms that support CRM, marketing, and transaction management, giving agents the flexibility and efficiency needed to thrive in modern real estate. These tools are cloud-based, allowing agents to access their work anytime, anywhere.

Compass is known for its proprietary technology, which is geared toward marketing and luxury markets. While it is highly regarded, the higher costs associated with accessing these tools may deter some agents from looking for a more affordable option.

Agent Satisfaction

LPT Realty has a Glassdoor rating of 4.3 stars from 37 reviews, with agents praising the revenue share program and lower fees. The smaller number of reviews reflects LPT Realty’s newer entry into the market, but feedback is generally positive.

Compass holds a 3.7-star rating from 2.4K reviews. While its prestige and technology are appreciated, many agents express concerns over the higher fees and the lack of income opportunities beyond commissions.

Final Thoughts on LPT Realty vs Compass

LPT Realty stands out with its lower fees, income opportunities through revenue sharing, and long-term financial benefits like retirement and willable income paths. It is a great option for agents looking for cost-effective solutions and passive income streams.

Compass is a prestigious brokerage with strong branding and luxury market expertise, but its higher costs and lack of passive income options may not appeal to agents focused on maximizing earnings and planning for the future.

title: Real vs Compass: Which Brokerage is Best for Realtors in 2025?
excerpt: Choosing between Real (also known as Real Broker) and Compass is a crucial decision for any real estate agent looking to build a successful career.…
content:
Choosing between Real (also known as Real Broker) and Compass is a crucial decision for any real estate agent looking to build a successful career. This article will break down the key differences in commission splits, fees, training, technology, and overall agent satisfaction to help you determine which brokerage better aligns with your goals.

Commission Splits and Fees
Income Opportunities
Training and Support
Technology and Resources
Agent Satisfaction
Final Thoughts on Real vs Compass
Join Our FREE Team at eXp Realty

Commission Splits and Fees

Real Broker offers highly competitive commission splits ranging from 85/15 to 100/0, with no franchise or royalty fees, making it a top choice for agents wanting to maximize their earnings. Real Broker also has a production cap of $12K, meaning that agents keep 100% of their commissions once they hit this cap. The brokerage also rewards top agents with a $24K bonus, making it appealing for high producers. Monthly fees are $750 per year, with no transaction fees until agents reach the cap.

Compass, on the other hand, offers commission splits from 60/40 to 92.5/7.5, but agents must pay a 4% marketing fee, which can make a significant cut into their overall earnings. Additionally, Compass only sometimes offers a production cap or top agent bonuses, which can be a drawback for high-earning agents. Monthly fees vary by office but typically start at $145.

Income Opportunities

One of the standout features of Real Broker is its revenue share model, which allows agents to earn income by recruiting other agents. This provides an additional income stream that goes beyond commissions. Real Broker also offers retirement income and willable income paths for agents who meet the time qualifications, giving them long-term financial security.

In contrast, Compass does not offer a revenue share program or additional income opportunities like retirement income or willable income. Agents at Compass are primarily dependent on their commission earnings, which may limit long-term wealth-building potential.

Training and Support

Real Broker excels in providing consistent, high-quality training. Agents have access to 30+ live training sessions each week, covering everything from lead generation to transaction management. Additionally, Real offers 24/7 agent support, ensuring that agents can get help whenever they need it.

Compass provides training through its Compass Academy, which is widely recognized for its quality. However, unlike Real Broker, Compass does not offer 24/7 support, which could be a limitation for agents working in fast-paced markets or requiring immediate assistance.

Technology and Resources

Both brokerages offer cutting-edge technology to help agents succeed. Real Broker uses cloud-based systems that integrate CRM tools, marketing platforms, and transaction management, making it highly efficient for tech-savvy agents. Compass, on the other hand, is known for its proprietary technology platform, which includes marketing, listing tools, and data-driven insights to help agents stand out. However, Compass’s tech offerings come at a higher cost, which could impact an agent’s profitability.

Agent Satisfaction

Real Broker has an impressive Glassdoor rating of 4.5 stars from 109 reviews. Agents at Real frequently praise the transparent fee structure, income opportunities, and the level of training and support offered.

Compass holds a 3.7-star rating from 2.4K reviews on Glassdoor. While Compass agents appreciate the brand’s marketing power and tech tools, complaints often arise around the high fees and limited income opportunities beyond commissions.

Final Thoughts on Real vs Compass

For agents looking for a brokerage with lower fees, more income streams, and robust support, Real Broker is the clear winner. The revenue share model, bonus opportunities, and comprehensive training make it a solid choice for agents who want to grow their careers while maximizing their earnings.

Compass offers a well-known brand and excellent technology, but higher fees and fewer income opportunities might make it less attractive for agents focused on long-term wealth-building.

For more in-depth comparisons, check out our eXp vs traditional brokerage comparison and online brokerage comparison pages. Explore the advantages each brokerage offers, and don’t forget to see why many agents are turning to eXp Realty for even more opportunities.

Selecting the right brokerage can significantly influence a real estate agent’s career path, financial gains, and overall satisfaction. In this analysis, we’ll dive into the key differences between Real vs Fathom Realty, two leading players in the real estate industry. We’ll examine their foundational elements, financial performance, commission splits, income opportunities, training, and support systems, along with additional features that each offers.

Overview
Financial Strength
Commission Splits and Fees
Income Opportunities
Training and Support
Additional Features
Final Thoughts on Real vs Fathom Realty
Join our FREE Team at eXp Realty

Overview

Real was founded in 2014 and has quickly become a notable contender in the real estate brokerage industry with its innovative approach and cloud-based model. The brokerage boasts over 20,000 agents across 50 states, indicating a significant presence in the U.S. real estate market. Real’s Glassdoor rating stands at 4.5 from 109 reviews, highlighting strong agent satisfaction and a positive work environment.

Fathom Realty, established in 2010, has developed a strong foundation with operations in 41 states and a network of over 11,000 agents. It enjoys a slightly higher Glassdoor rating of 4.6 from 425 reviews, reflecting a strong reputation for agent satisfaction and a supportive company culture.

Both Real and Fathom Realty have carved out unique niches within the real estate sector, with Real focusing on rapid growth and technological solutions, while Fathom Realty emphasizes its extensive reach and agent-centric policies.

Financial Strength

When evaluating financial strength in Real vs Fathom Realty, there is a distinct contrast between the two. Real, listed under the ticker REAL, trades around $6 and has had only 1 profitable quarter out of 19. This suggests challenges in achieving consistent profitability, which might raise concerns for agents seeking stable, long-term financial growth.

Fathom Realty, on the other hand, is publicly listed under the ticker FATH, trading around $2. Similar to Real, Fathom has also experienced only 1 profitable quarter, indicating financial hurdles in maintaining steady profitability.

Both brokerages face financial challenges, but Fathom Realty has a more transparent track record due to its longer tenure in the industry. In contrast, Real’s position as a newer player might imply potential for growth, albeit with some financial instability.

Commission Splits and Fees

Commission splits and associated fees are pivotal factors in deciding between brokerages. Fathom Realty offers a 100/0 commission split, which is appealing to agents seeking to maximize their earnings without sharing a portion with the brokerage. This structure, however, comes without any top agent bonuses. Fathom Realty operates with a production cap of $0, which is advantageous for agents who wish to retain most of their commission earnings.

Real, meanwhile, provides an 85/15 to 100/0 commission split, allowing agents to start with a modest commission share and gradually earn more as they cap. Real’s production cap is set at $12K, which could be appealing for agents who can quickly reach this cap and enjoy higher commission retention. Additionally, Real offers a $24K elite agent bonus, serving as an incentive for high-performing agents to maximize their income potential.

When comparing commission splits in Real vs Fathom Realty, Fathom’s 100/0 split offers agents the maximum possible commission retention, but lacks additional bonuses. Real’s flexible split structure, combined with bonus opportunities, might attract agents looking for a balanced approach between initial commission sharing and eventual high earnings.

Income Opportunities

Beyond traditional commissions, income opportunities can significantly impact an agent’s earnings. Real provides multiple avenues for supplementary income, including revenue sharing and stock awards. Agents can earn 150 shares upon annual capping and have the option to purchase discounted stock with a 5% pre-cap bonus and a 20% post-cap bonus. These features offer substantial financial growth potential, especially for agents interested in building wealth through stock ownership.

In contrast, Fathom Realty does not provide revenue share income or stock awards, focusing solely on commission earnings. This lack of additional income streams might limit financial opportunities for agents who prefer diversified earning potential beyond commissions.

The analysis of income opportunities in Real vs Fathom Realty highlights Real’s edge in offering revenue sharing and stock awards, which could be appealing for agents seeking to expand their income sources. Fathom Realty’s straightforward commission structure may suit those who prioritize simplicity over-diversification.

Training and Support

Training and support are vital aspects of any brokerage, significantly influencing an agent’s professional development and success. Real offers 30+ live training hours per week, ensuring agents have access to continuous learning and skill enhancement opportunities. However, it does not provide a virtual office environment, which could limit access to collaborative tools.

Fathom Realty, similar to Real, does not offer a virtual office setup or structured live training sessions. While Fathom emphasizes agent satisfaction and community support, its lack of comprehensive training programs might be a drawback for agents accustomed to regular educational offerings.

In the comparison of training and support between Real vs Fathom Realty, Real edges out slightly with its provision of live training sessions, albeit without a virtual office. Fathom’s higher Glassdoor rating might indicate stronger cultural satisfaction and support, but both brokerages have room for improvement in their training offerings.

Additional Features

Additional features such as healthcare options, mentorship programs, and technological tools can significantly enhance an agent’s experience at a brokerage. Real offers healthcare options, which can be crucial for agents seeking comprehensive benefits beyond financial compensation. However, it lacks mentorship programs and does not provide a free sponsor value or partner networks to the extent seen in other brokerages.

Fathom Realty, in contrast, offers healthcare options but also lacks mentorship programs and additional technological tools. The absence of these features might be a drawback for agents seeking a full-service brokerage with comprehensive support systems.

In evaluating additional features in Real vs Fathom Realty, both brokerages have specific strengths but also areas where they could enhance their offerings. Real’s healthcare options and stock purchase programs may attract agents looking for added benefits, while Fathom’s established reputation and agent-centric culture could appeal to those seeking community support.

Final Thoughts on Real vs Fathom Realty

The choice between Real vs Fathom Realty hinges on individual agent priorities and career aspirations. Real offers a balanced approach with flexible commission splits, revenue sharing, and stock awards, making it attractive to agents seeking diversified income opportunities and growth potential. Its innovative approach and extensive training programs cater to agents who value professional development and financial incentives. To view our full chart comparing the top online real estate brokerages click here.

On the other hand, Fathom Realty provides a straightforward 100/0 commission split, focusing on maximizing agent earnings without additional complexities. Its established presence and agent-centric culture provide stability and support, which can be appealing for agents prioritizing simplicity and community engagement.

In weighing the factors of Real vs Fathom Realty, agents should consider which brokerage aligns best with their financial goals, support needs, and overall career vision. Both brokerages offer unique advantages, and the decision ultimately rests on whether an agent values diversified income streams and training or prefers a no-commission-sharing approach with a strong community focus.
